title: Painting a front door in exterior gloss
subtitle: Strip, fill, prime, and top-coat a wooden front door to a durable exterior finish
excerpt: How to repaint a wooden front door in oil-based or water-based exterior gloss. Covers rubbing down the existing paint, filling, priming bare timber, the correct painting order, and how to keep the finish clean while the door is back in use.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Johnstone's Paint technical guidance (generic guidance on oil-based application, referenced not reproduced).

glossaryTerms.definition: A traditional solvent-based paint with an alkyd resin binder. Slower to dry than water-based alternatives (4 to 6 hours between coats), harder to clean brushes, but builds a denser film that is more resistant to water ingress on exterior joinery.

The sequence for painting a panelled door: mouldings first, panels second, muntin and middle rail third, stiles and remaining rails last. Working in this order avoids brush marks where sections overlap.

content.text: A front door takes more wear than any other painted surface in the house, sun, rain, frost, and hands every day. The paint life depends almost entirely on preparation. A door that is properly rubbed down, primed on every bare edge, and given two full top coats will outlast a door that is just repainted over a flaking surface by 5 or 6 years.
Step 1, Choose the right day

Do not paint in direct sun, in temperatures below 10°C, or when rain is forecast within 4 hours. Oil-based gloss on a sun-heated door surface skins quickly and then stays tacky underneath for days. Water-based exterior gloss needs 4°C minimum and should not be applied when condensation is forming on the door surface.
Step 2, Rub down and strip flaking paint

Scrape off any loose or flaking paint with the 50 mm scraper. Work firmly but at a low angle, gouging into the timber creates work to fill later. Sand the whole door with 80-grit paper on the sanding block: you are not stripping to bare wood, but breaking the gloss on the existing paint so the new coats bond. Finish with 120-grit to smooth out the scratch marks. Take particular care to sand the top and bottom edges of the door, these are the most exposed surfaces and are often missed.
Step 3, Fill and prime bare timber

Fill any holes, splits, or gaps in the timber with an exterior wood filler or a two-part wood filler for larger repairs. Sand flush when hard. Prime all areas of exposed bare timber with an oil-based wood primer. The primer seals the wood grain and provides a surface the top coats bond to. Bare timber top-coated without primer shows raised grain within a year and peels within two.
Step 4, Mask and apply the undercoat
Mask the glazing with masking tape and press the tape edges down firmly. Apply one coat of oil-based undercoat in the correct
painting order

: mouldings first (the 25 mm brush), panels second, then the structural rails and stiles with the 50 mm brush. Keep a wet edge, start each brush stroke in an unpainted area and drag into the wet paint to avoid lap marks. Allow the undercoat to dry fully (at least 16 hours for oil-based; 2 to 4 hours for water-based) before the next coat.
Step 5, Top coat
Lightly sand the undercoat with 180-grit paper and wipe off the dust before applying the first
oil-based gloss

top coat. Apply in the same painting order. Lay off each section with light parallel strokes in the direction of the grain; do not go back over drying paint. Allow at least 12 hours (ideally overnight) before the second top coat. Two thin coats last longer than one thick one.
Step 6, Refit and cure

Remove the masking tape before the final coat has fully hardened, pulling tape off a hard gloss film can lift the paint edge. Leave the door ajar overnight if possible while the final coat sets, or peel away any point where the door has stuck to the frame before closing it for the first time. Oil-based gloss takes up to a week to reach full hardness.
Variations

Water-based exterior gloss: faster drying and lower odour. The preparation is identical. Water-based systems can be more flexible but build a slightly thinner film per coat; three coats rather than two gives equivalent protection.
Heavily weathered or stripped door: apply knotting solution to any knots before priming, then use two coats of primer before the undercoat.
Care

A gloss-painted door needs repainting every 5 to 7 years in a sheltered position; every 3 to 5 years on a south or west-facing elevation. The trigger is when the surface starts to chalk (rubs off on a white cloth) or shows hairline cracks in the flat sections. Chalking and crazing spread under the surface, catching the door at this stage avoids a full strip-to-bare-wood job.
Troubleshooter

items.symptom: Paint stays tacky for days after application
items.cause: Painted in direct sun on a warm door, so the surface skinned before the underlying paint dried
items.fix: Do not apply further coats until the paint has hardened. In future, paint in the morning before direct sun reaches the door, or on a cloudy day.

items.symptom: Brush marks visible in finished coats
items.cause: Brush marks laid off across the grain, or going back over paint that had started to skin
items.fix: Sand out the marks with 180-grit, wipe off dust, and recoat, laying off along the grain in a single direction.

items.symptom: Paint peeling at door edges within a year
items.cause: Top edges and bottom rail not primed; water entered the end grain and lifted the paint from below
items.fix: Strip back to bare timber at the edges, apply two coats of primer, and top coat as above.
